label: Localized watch band contact rash without infection or anaphylaxis
summary: Your symptoms fit a localized skin reaction from watch band exposure.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a localized watch band contact skin reaction. We did not find anaphylaxis, mucosal involvement, skin infection, or another rash emergency today.
home: Avoid the suspected trigger if it is known.
Wash gently with mild soap and water, and avoid scrubbing the rash.
Use cool compresses, moisturizers, or coverings only as your clinician recommended.
meds: Use creams, ointments, itch medicine, or allergy medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use leftover steroid, antibiotic, or allergy medicine unless your clinician told you to.
return: Trouble breathing, throat tightness, swelling of the lips or tongue, fainting, or a bodywide reaction.
Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, rapidly worsening rash, skin peeling, or mouth sores.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, allergy, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized watch band contact skin reaction.
No anaphylaxis, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal involvement, skin infection, severe medication reaction, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, hypotension, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal lesions, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, skin infection, rapidly progressive rash, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology/allergy plan.

terms: clinician diagnosis localized watch band contact rash
discharge diagnosis localized watch band contact rash
localized watch band contact rash diagnosed

high_confidence_terms: clinician diagnosis localized watch band contact rash
localized watch band contact rash diagnosed
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports contact dermatitis framing from irritant or allergic exposures, trigger avoidance, gentle skin care, clinician-directed topical or allergy treatment, and escalation for severe allergic, infectious, ocular, or mucosal red flags. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed localized watch band contact skin reaction with anaphylaxis, infection, mucosal, severe-rash, pregnancy/pediatric, and specialist-directed pathways absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of localized watch band contact skin reaction
no infection, mucosal, or severe-rash red flags

label: Dry cracked feet without diabetic-foot or infection red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit dry, cracked skin on the feet.
found: Your clinician diagnosed dry, cracked feet. We did not find spreading infection, open ulcer, diabetic-foot risk, poor circulation, or a systemic skin emergency today.
home: Use a gentle moisturizer as your clinician recommended.
Avoid harsh soaps, irritating chemicals, and friction when you can.
Keep the skin clean and protected from new cracking.
meds: Use skin medicines only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use leftover antibiotic or steroid creams unless your clinician said they are safe.
return: Spreading redness, pus, fever, red streaks, warmth, or worsening pain.
Black skin, numbness, poor circulation, a new foot ulcer, or bleeding cracks that do not heal.
Skin peeling, mouth sores, widespread rash, or symptoms that worsen.
follow: Follow up with primary care, podiatry, urgent care, dermatology, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of dry cracked feet or localized xerosis.
No secondary infection, open wound/ulcer, diabetic-foot/PVD/neuropathy risk, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Secondary infection, open wound or foot ulcer, diabetic foot, PVD, neuropathy, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, mucosal lesions, systemic illness, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology/podiatry plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus dry skin material supports moisturizer use, avoiding irritants, and seeking care for cracks, bleeding, infection signs, or persistent symptoms. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed localized dry cracked feet without infection, open wound, diabetic-foot/PVD/neuropathy risk, systemic skin disease, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of dry cracked feet
